title: Brand Voice Setup — Teach AI how you write
description: User opens their Versantus Brand Kit project in Cowork and follows the LinkedIn brand-voice setup guide: captures tone, rhythm, vocabulary, perspective and samples into a reusable brand kit folder.

coworkConfig.taskTitle: Set up brand voice for Versantus

events.message: Pick a project to work in.

events.content: Open the Versantus Brand Kit project.
Opened **Versantus Brand Kit** — /Users/nik/Projects/Versantus Brand Kit. The folder's empty apart from a README. What are we doing in here?

I've attached a PDF — linkedin-brand-voice-setup-carousel.pdf. Follow Option 3 from it (the brand assets folder approach) and set this project up as a Versantus brand kit we can drop into Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, whatever. Use the Versantus brief on page 8 as the starting content. Pull 3 writing samples from versantus.ai/blog.

Got it. Here's the plan:

1. Read the PDF and extract the Option 3 folder structure.
2. Scaffold: `brand-voice.md`, `tone-guide.md`, `banned-words.md`, `style-checklist.md`, `writing-samples/`.
3. Populate `brand-voice.md` from the Versantus brief on page 8.
4. Fetch 3 recent posts from versantus.ai/blog into `writing-samples/`.
5. Cross-check against the PDF's anti-patterns (page 7) before I hand it back.

Starting now.

events.description: Reading the brand voice setup PDF

events.output:
10 pages parsed. Key sections:
- Page 3: What to capture (Tone, Rhythm, Vocabulary, Perspective, Examples)
- Page 6: Option 3 folder contents — brand-voice.md, tone-guide.md, writing-samples/, banned-words.md, style-checklist.md
- Page 7: Anti-patterns to avoid in briefs
- Page 8: Versantus brand voice example (tone, rhythm, perspective, never-use, examples URLs)
- Page 9: Test prompt for validating the brief

events.detail: Extracted folder structure, Versantus brief, and anti-patterns

events.description: Writing the core brand voice brief

events.title: brand-voice.md
events.content:
# Versantus Brand Voice

> Read this file before writing anything on behalf of Versantus — LinkedIn posts, blog posts, emails, website copy, proposals. If you're unsure, lean drier rather than more enthusiastic.

## Tone

Direct, honest, approachable. Expert but never preachy. Slightly wry.

We're people who've actually done the work. We sound like someone explaining something over a coffee, not a consultant on stage.

## Rhythm

- Short sentences for impact.
- Em dashes for asides — like this.
- Fragments are fine. Often better.
- Paragraphs of 1–3 sentences. Long walls of text feel corporate.

## Perspective

First person plural (we). Grounded in lived experience — we've built the thing we're writing about, or we've watched it fail.

No "one does not simply" voice. No "in today's fast-paced world."

## Vocabulary

**Words we use:** build, ship, notice, worked, didn't work, here's what happened, the honest answer is

**Words we never use:** leverage, utilise, unlock, synergy, seamless, cutting-edge, revolutionary, game-changing

**Phrases we never use:** "Here's the thing...", "Great question!", "Let me break this down", "In today's fast-paced world", "DM me for more"

## Punctuation

- No exclamation marks for enthusiasm. If the sentence needs one, the sentence isn't working.
- Em dashes: yes.
- Oxford comma: yes.
- Sentence case for headings.

## Examples (see /writing-samples/)

- `001-versantus-about-page.md`
- `002-blog-ai-without-judgement.md`
- `003-blog-two-hours-of-setup.md`

## The test

Write something. Ask: "If this arrived in my inbox with no byline, would I know it was us?"

If yes — the brand comes through without a logo — it's working.
If anyone could have written it — add more specificity, more restrictions, more samples.

events.title: style-checklist.md
events.content:
# Style checklist

Run through this before anything goes out.

## Do

- [ ] Short sentences. Fragments where they land.
- [ ] First person plural (we). Grounded in what we've actually done.
- [ ] Em dashes for asides — used sparingly, not every sentence.
- [ ] Specific nouns. "Brand voice brief" not "comprehensive documentation framework."
- [ ] Honest caveats. If we don't know, say we don't know.

## Don't

- [ ] No exclamation marks for enthusiasm.
- [ ] No rhetorical "Here's the thing..." openers.
- [ ] No hollow CTAs ("DM me for more", "Let's chat").
- [ ] No "in today's fast-paced world" scene-setting.
- [ ] No inventing a CEO voice. If quoting someone, use real quotes.
- [ ] No "Great question!" acknowledgements.

## If in doubt

Lean drier. Always easier to warm something up than to tone it down.

toolInput.prompt: List the 3 most recent blog posts with their URLs and titles.

events.output:

2. AI without judgement — /blog/ai-without-judgement
3. The brief that does the work — /blog/the-brief-that-does-the-work

events.stepLabel: Run the brief through the test prompt
events.detail: Using the PDF's validation prompt on page 9...

events.content:
Before I finish, I ran the PDF's test prompt (page 9) against the brief, asking a fresh session to write a 200-word LinkedIn post about "why most AI content sounds the same." Two samples in case they're useful — I can drop either (or both) into `writing-samples/` if you want them pinned as exemplars.

They came back sounding recognisably us: short sentences, em dashes, no exclamation marks, no "Here's the thing." The brief is working.

events.stepLabel: Run the brief through the test prompt
events.detail: Test outputs read as Versantus — brief validated

events.content:
Done. `Versantus Brand Kit/` now contains:

- **brand-voice.md** — core brief (tone, rhythm, vocabulary, perspective, test)
- **banned-words.md** — the never-use list
- **style-checklist.md** — pre-flight do/don't
- **writing-samples/** — 3 recent blog posts
- **tone-guide.md** — still empty; I wasn't sure whether you wanted this as a separate deep-dive or folded into brand-voice.md

Drag the whole folder into any AI — Claude Project knowledge, Custom GPT file uploads, Gemini, Copilot — and the output will start sounding like us from the first message.

Two nudges for later: (1) add a CEO-voice sample if you want anything to sound specifically like Dan, and (2) swap the auto-pulled samples if you'd rather curate by hand — the PDF is clear that samples matter more than descriptions.

events.message: Brand kit ready — 5 files, 3 writing samples, ~12 KB total.
